headline: Sabah paused a federal rollout. Federal courts paused Sabah's 40% payout.
context: On 13 May 2026, Sabah deferred the federal border agency (AKPS) rollout citing MA63 immigration autonomy. On 6 April 2026, the Court of Appeal granted Putrajaya a stay on Sabah's 40% revenue ruling. Both delays invoke the same 1963 agreement.

cards.big: Sabah is now refusing one federal rollout. Putrajaya has been pausing one Sabah ruling for weeks.
cards.sub: On 13 May Sabah deferred the AKPS border agency. On 6 April the Court of Appeal stayed Sabah's 40% revenue order. Both sides cite MA63.

cards.big: On 6 April 2026 the Court of Appeal granted Putrajaya's stay of execution on Sabah's 40% revenue ruling.
cards.sub: The Attorney General's Chambers said the formula was 'untouched' — but the payment timeline is frozen pending appeal. Sabah had prevailed at the earlier tier.

cards.big: On 13 May 2026, CM Hajiji Noor postponed the Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency (AKPS) rollout in Sabah.
cards.sub: Hajiji said Sabah needed direct state control over AKPS — under MA63 immigration autonomy — before the agency could deploy.

cards.big: The Sabah Law Society and ex-CM Salleh Said Keruak both publicly backed the AKPS deferral within 48 hours.
cards.sub: SLS framed the delay as constitutional caution; Salleh said the call was about balancing federal ties with MA63 safeguards.

cards.big: When Putrajaya pauses a Sabah ruling, it is called 'due process.' When Sabah pauses a Putrajaya rollout, it is called 'obstruction.'
Both delays cite the same 1963 agreement. Only one comes with a court order. The other comes with public criticism.
